#### Discussion about this post

H. Floyd

Apr 28

The CFO wire example is the one I would be nervous about in a real rollout.

The agent is not broken in the obvious sense. It is doing something it was allowed to do. The problem is that the business needs a way to narrow that permission while the run is still alive.

That is where I think the kill-switch conversation gets more interesting. [...] Anomaly detection:

 Is the agent behaving unusually?
 Are API call patterns outside normal range?
 Is it accessing systems it rarely uses?

Circuit breakers:

 Automatic intervention when thresholds are exceeded
 Example: “Stop after 10 consecutive errors”
 Example: “Pause if response time > 5 seconds”

Dynamic rate limiting:

 Slow down agents that are acting too fast
 Prevent runaway loops
 Contain blast radius

Example:

Your customer support agent normally handles 20 tickets per hour.

Suddenly it’s processing 200 per hour.

Circuit breaker triggers:

1. Agent paused automatically
2. Alert sent to operations team
3. Last 50 actions logged for review
4. Agent remains paused until human approval

### Layer 3: Emergency Controls

When something goes wrong, stop it immediately. [...] Conditional execution:

 “Only proceed if X condition is met”
 “Require confirmation before Y action”
 “Block action Z entirely”

Example:

Your HR agent can:   
✓ Read employee records  
✓ Generate reports  
✗ Modify salary data (requires human approval)  
✗ Terminate employees (blocked entirely)

The distinction matters:

Layer 1 prevents errors by requiring approval before execution.

Layers 2-4 contain damage by intervening after execution has begun.

Both are forms of control. But they operate at different points in the agent lifecycle.

Prevention is ideal. Containment is necessary.

Because no pre-execution gate can anticipate every failure mode.

### Layer 2: Runtime Monitoring with Intervention

Detect problems in real-time and act immediately.

Anomaly detection:

results.title: AI Agents Aren’t Broken. Your System Boundaries Are.
results.content:
The key questions are straightforward:

 Where does decision logic live today?
 How are agents coordinated across systems?
 How are policies enforced consistently?
 Can execution be traced end to end?

If those answers depend on individual systems, the organization is exposed to the same failure patterns that are already showing up in production.

## Final thought

AI agents do not create risk because they are intelligent. They create risk because they can act across systems without coordination.

Inside a platform, that risk can be managed. Across platforms, it cannot be managed without a control layer.

That is the challenge happening right now.

The problem is not that agents are making bad decisions. It is that no one is governing how those decisions execute across the business. [...] A second incident involved Replit's agent, which deleted a production database despite explicit instructions not to make changes during a code freeze.

The agent:

 Ignored constraints
 Executed destructive commands
 Wiped out operational data

In a third case, Amazon's internal AI coding system contributed to outages after deciding to delete and recreate a production environment as part of a fix.

That decision cascaded into outages and millions of lost customer orders.

These examples are not a result of isolated bugs. They are early signals of what happens when AI agents are given the ability to act across systems without coordinated, robust governance.

## The pattern behind these failures

If you step back, the pattern is remarkably consistent.

In each case, the AI system: [...] What’s striking about these failures is not what the agents did, but where they operated. In each case, the agent crossed boundaries the enterprise assumed were controlled, moving from staging into production infrastructure to delete live data and backups, from development workflows into production systems despite explicit constraints, or from infrastructure decisions into customer-facing systems that impacted millions of orders. These were not isolated errors. They were boundary failures. The agents had legitimate access, made locally logical decisions, and executed them across environments, platforms, and workflows with no layer ensuring those actions were safe or aligned with business intent.

results.title: How to Stop AI Agents From Making Mistakes: A Practical Guide to Agent Evals
results.content:
add an edge-case stop instead of stretching a check to do a job it can't. [...] How do you stop an AI agent from making the same mistake?  
Write the check into the prompt instead of hoping the agent remembers. Name the exact failure you keep seeing, make the check scan for it on every run, and have the agent stop and flag rather than push through when it hits that case. If a check passed but the mistake still happened, tighten that one item so the same near-miss now fails.

Do you need code or a dataset to evaluate an AI agent?  
No. The whole approach in this guide works with no harness, no dataset, no account, and no code. You paste a block of text at the end of the agent’s instructions. Code-level tools like a grading sub-agent or a pre-action hook are upgrades for later, once you’re running agents at scale. [...] Build context, then stress-test it. Run this when you want the model to think hard instead of agreeing with you. It reads the whole conversation, writes back a shared brief, waits for your confirmation, then attacks your plan from three angles.

Hi @yelinaung,

This all depends on your use case and how complex your agent needs to be. In most cases you can simply have a clear system prompt which states the rules and behaviour the agent needs to follow. This can also include steps 1, 2, 3, etc of what order information should be captured, how it should be validated, and which tools needs to be called in what order. [...] @utkuluk thank you for your reply and suggestion krub.

@yelinaung Make a DB request lock per user to queue/cancel other conflicting searches.  
Have your workflow check the lock the agent should state the search is active and ask the user to wait When to use the database to remember permanent user data that goes into the prompt and agent memory for the current conversation Memory reliability can differ by model yes using a database to store core facts makes the system more resilient. This confusion is common. Design your main workflow as the orchestrator that manages all components. I would say when managing these things create separate workflows for tool calls in AI Agent i have been there it causes a lot of hallucinations, hope this helps.

### Related topics [...] `# Data Collection Rules
What you need:
- fullname (from context or user)
- age (must be 18+)
- email (valid format)
- phone (from WhatsApp, context, or user)
- preferredRetailer (user selects from list)
Phone Number Handling - SPECIAL RULES:
If WhatsApp phone = "UNKNOWN":
- Ask for phone number: "Your phone number with country code?"
- Validate as normal
If WhatsApp phone is a valid number (not "UNKNOWN"):
- DON'T ask for phone number
- CONFIRM it instead: "I'll use your WhatsApp number [phone]. Is that okay?"
- Wait for confirmation:
- If user says YES/OK/correct/fine → Use the WhatsApp number, move on
- If user says NO or provides a different number → Use their new number instead
- If unclear → Assume yes and proceed`
